behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: A Unit test of the validator alone proves validation works, but misses whether row numbers are preserved through the CSV-reading → validation → DLQ-writing pipeline. The failure could be in the integration point (row number lost during transition). Testing each component separately won't catch this; we need CSV reading, validation, and DLQ writing wired together.
behaviors.test_description: Read a CSV with rows missing required fields, verify that (1) validation rejects them, (2) each rejected row appears in the DLQ, and (3) the DLQ record includes the original row number from the input file. Use mocked S3 and DLQ, real CSV parser and validator.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: This is pure function logic with no I/O or external dependencies. A parameterized Unit test calling the transformation function directly with different date formats can catch all real failures (wrong parsing, wrong output format, missed edge cases). Mocks aren't needed—we're testing the transformation itself, not its integration into the pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Parameterized Unit test: for each date format (ISO 8601, MM/DD/YYYY, DD/MM/YYYY, edge cases like leap years), call the transformation function and assert the output matches the canonical format (e.g., YYYY-MM-DD). No CSV reader, no pipeline needed.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: Real failures here depend on OS-level resource behavior (memory allocation, process scheduling, timeout enforcement). A Unit test with 100 rows or mocked I/O will always pass, hiding OOM and timeout bugs. You must process a real-scale data volume through the actual validation and transformation logic. Mocking S3 and PostgreSQL writes is acceptable (to avoid network latency), but the CSV parsing and validation must be real and must handle 500K rows.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: generate or mock a 500K-row CSV stream from S3 (mocked), process rows through real validation and transformation logic, mock PostgreSQL writes, measure memory and elapsed time. Verify memory usage stays below limit and processing completes within timeout. Use a profiler or memory tracking to catch leaks in the validation loop.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: With careful mocking, Unit tests can catch this: mock the S3 client to return 503 twice then succeed, mock time.sleep() to track call arguments, and verify the retry loop calls sleep with exponential intervals (1, 2, 4 seconds). No OS-level infrastructure needed—this is pure retry logic. A mock will not miss the failure if you verify both retry count and sleep timing. (Integration with moto is also valid but not necessary.)
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with mocks: patch S3 client to raise ClientError('503') on first two calls, succeed on third. Patch time.sleep() to record arguments. Call pipeline.read_from_s3(). Assert S3 client was called 3 times and time.sleep() was called with arguments [1, 2, 4] (or [2^0, 2^1, 2^2]), confirming exponential backoff.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: This depends on database transaction semantics and primary key constraints—both PostgreSQL features. Mocking PostgreSQL would hide the failure: a mock could silently accept duplicate keys, or a mock might incorrectly allow the duplication that the real DB prevents. You need a real (test) database instance to verify idempotency and constraint behavior. S3 and CSV reading can be mocked, but PostgreSQL must be real.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: (1) start pipeline, insert first batch of rows into real PostgreSQL, (2) simulate crash/restart mid-batch, (3) restart pipeline and re-insert rows, (4) verify total row count equals rows from both batches (no duplicates), and verify no primary key constraint violations. Use a real local PostgreSQL test instance; mock S3 to return CSV data and log row positions.
